title: A fin-loop-like structure in GPX4 underlies neuroprotection from ferroptosis
theme: GPX4 membrane anchoring
conditionVector: Patient fibroblast-derived iPSC neurons and forebrain organoids, plus inducible Camk2a and L7Cre mouse models; ferroptosis is unmasked by liproxstatin-1 withdrawal or conditional Gpx4 deletion rather than by an added inducer; readouts are live-cell imaging, NMR and crystallography, bicelle and GUV binding, MRI and plasma neurofilament light over days to weeks.

sixtySecond.story: GPX4 is a soluble enzyme that has to work on hydroperoxides buried in a membrane, and how it reaches them was unresolved.
sixtySecond.advance: A patient GPX4 variant, p.R152H, collapses a hydrophobic fin-loop that anchors the enzyme to the bilayer while leaving catalytic activity close to wild type in the purified assay used here, which separates catalysis from membrane access as two distinct requirements.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: The same variant is followed through three human patients, gene-corrected iPSC neurons and forebrain organoids, NMR and crystal structures, defined-lipid vesicle binding, and two neuron-specific mouse models.
sixtySecond.scope: This is a very rare homozygous hypomorphic allele studied in engineered human cells and strong conditional mouse genetics; the Alzheimer-like proteomic overlap is a similarity measurement, not a cause of sporadic Alzheimer disease.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: Can membrane access be restored pharmacologically without raising global GPX4 catalytic activity, and is a measurable localisation defect present in common neurodegeneration rather than only a similar proteomic endpoint?

figureAudit.question: Is the patient GPX4 R152H allele sufficient to cause degeneration in human neural cells that ferroptosis inhibition can reverse?
figureAudit.intervention: Patient-derived homozygous neurons and forebrain organoids, CRISPR correction of the allele, and withdrawal or re-supply of liproxstatin-1.
figureAudit.readout: Clinical imaging in three patients, live-cell death imaging and organoid degeneration.
figureAudit.answer: The patients show progressive cerebellar and partial cortical atrophy; homozygous neurons and organoids degenerate when liproxstatin-1 is withdrawn, and both gene correction and inhibitor re-supply rescue them.
figureAudit.boundary: Early organoid culture depended on antioxidant support, so this models a rare homozygous hypomorph rather than the natural course of a common neurodegenerative disease.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does R152H fail because the protein is lost or catalytically dead, or because of a previously unrecognised structural defect?
figureAudit.intervention: Purification of GPX4 R152H from mammalian cells, NMR relaxation and chemical-shift analysis, and crystallography.
figureAudit.readout: LC-MS/MS reduction of phosphatidylcholine hydroperoxide, HSQC and heteronuclear NOE, and the variant structure.
figureAudit.answer: R152H reduces the hydroperoxide substrate at close to wild-type rates, but loses stabilisation of N132 and A133 so that I129 occupies the site and the protruding fin-loop collapses.
figureAudit.boundary: Normal catalytic activity applies only to this purified system and substrate; it does not measure the localisation-limited reaction rate on an intact cell membrane.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does the hydrophobic tip of the fin-loop carry membrane binding, and can that be separated from catalysis?
figureAudit.intervention: Bicelle NMR, I129S and L130S substitutions, R152K, and rescue of GPX4-null cells.
figureAudit.readout: Binding to bicelles and phosphatidylserine-containing giant vesicles, membrane fractionation, TIRF, Airyscan and SIM imaging, hydroperoxide activity and cell survival.
figureAudit.answer: Wild-type protein binds membranes while R152H and the double-serine mutant bind far less; the double-serine mutant keeps its fold and in vitro activity yet cannot rescue GPX4-null cells, and R152K partially restores both structure and function.
figureAudit.boundary: Fractionation and microscopy report membrane abundance and nanoscale clustering, not single-molecule residence time, and the vesicle lipid composition is simpler than a neuronal membrane.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does neuronal GPX4 loss or R152H hypofunction drive cortical and cerebellar degeneration in the mature brain?
figureAudit.intervention: Inducible Camk2a neuronal models, an L7Cre cerebellar model, and a ten-day liproxstatin-1 course.
figureAudit.readout: Serial MRI, Nissl and TUNEL staining, neuronal and glial markers, and plasma neurofilament light.
figureAudit.answer: Cortical atrophy, neuronal death, rising neurofilament light and secondary gliosis appear in the inducible model, Purkinje cells are lost in the cerebellar model, and short-term liproxstatin-1 lowers plasma neurofilament light.
figureAudit.boundary: The rescue is a short-term biomarker readout; recovery of brain volume, behaviour or survival was not shown, and conditional deletion is a strong genetic stress.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Do the molecular changes in GPX4-deficient brain overlap measurably with human Alzheimer datasets?
figureAudit.intervention: Neuroproteomics across degeneration time points in knockout and R152H models, compared with eight human Alzheimer proteomic datasets.
figureAudit.readout: Pathway enrichment and directionally consistent protein and network overlap.
figureAudit.answer: Both models enrich Alzheimer and other neurodegeneration pathways as degeneration progresses and share a subset of directionally consistent proteins with the human datasets.
figureAudit.boundary: Pathway names and proteomic overlap are disease similarity; they do not show that GPX4 deficiency causes sporadic Alzheimer disease, and no amyloid or tau pathology is demonstrated in these mice.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: Cytochrome P450 oxidoreductase contributes to phospholipid peroxidation in ferroptosis

versionEvents.note: Read in full on 2026-07-24. The notice corrects the catalogue information for one antibody in the Methods section headed "immunoblot analysis": the POR antibody recorded as Abcam ab133303 (clone UOTR1B493, lot GR290350-7) should be Abcam ab180597, 0.076 mg/mL, rabbit mAb to cytochrome P450 reductase, clone EPR 14479(B), lot GR148691-9, used at 1:1,000. It closes with "The errors have been corrected" and makes no statement about any figure, result or conclusion, so nothing about the paper's conclusions is either confirmed or withdrawn by it. Because the correction is confined to reagent identity, it changes which antibody a reader must order to reproduce the POR immunoblots in Fig. 2c, Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d.
versionEvents.supersededReagent: Abcam ab133303, rabbit monoclonal, clone UOTR1B493, lot GR290350-7, 1:1,000
versionEvents.correctedReagent: Abcam ab180597, 0.076 mg/mL, rabbit mAb to cytochrome P450 reductase, clone EPR 14479(B), lot GR148691-9, 1:1,000

reviews.checkedBy: Claude Code round-4 primary-source pass (implementer, not an independent reviewer)
reviews.sourceVersion: Nature Chemical Biology volume 17, page 501 (2021); Author Correction published 02 March 2021; publisher HTML retrieved in full 2026-07-24
reviews.boundary: Complete for the notice itself. It is not a reading of the corrected article. The notice names the Methods section it fixes but names no figure, so the mapping onto the POR immunoblot panels is inference, not something the notice states. It makes no claim about conclusions and none should be read into it.

theme: redox flux into phospholipid peroxidation
conditionVector: 786-O and UACC-257 and further carcinoma lines in culture, with ML210, RSL3, erastin, FIN56, BSO and FINO2 as inducers, some screens run after supplementing individual omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids; readouts are genome-wide CRISPR suppressor screens, C11-BODIPY, malondialdehyde and targeted LC-MS of oxidised phosphatidylethanolamines over acute dose-response windows.

sixtySecond.story: Phospholipid hydroperoxide accumulation is the rate-limiting event in ferroptosis, but the enzymes that feed it were poorly defined.
sixtySecond.advance: Unbiased suppressor screens identify cytochrome P450 oxidoreductase as functionally required for death across lineages and across several mechanistically distinct inducers, and lipidomics places its contribution at the peroxidation step rather than at substrate supply.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Multiple independent guides, clones and lineages, rescue by re-expression, and a lipidomic profile in which the global lipidome is largely unchanged while oxidised arachidonoyl and adrenoyl phosphatidylethanolamines fall.
sixtySecond.scope: All causal work is in cultured cancer cells under pharmacological GPX4 stress, some of it after exogenous fatty-acid loading; there is no in vivo flux measurement.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: Which acceptor actually receives electrons from POR to start the lethal phospholipid oxidation, and is the effect direct radical initiation or an indirect change in iron and haem cycling?

figureAudit.question: Can an unbiased genetic screen find a shared pro-ferroptotic redox node in both intrinsically sensitive and fatty-acid-sensitised states?
figureAudit.intervention: Genome-wide CRISPR suppressor screens in intrinsically sensitive 786-O cells and in UACC-257 cells supplemented with several polyunsaturated fatty acids, with ML210 and RSL3 dose-response.
figureAudit.readout: Enrichment of resistance hits, calibrated against the known ACSL4 hit.
figureAudit.answer: POR and ACSL4 recur as resistance hits across both settings and six fatty acids, while oleic acid does not sensitise in the same way.
figureAudit.boundary: The screen shows functional requirement in these cultured cells under GPX4 inhibition; it does not show that POR is a direct peroxidase, and supplementation conditions do not represent physiological lipid exposure.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Does the resistance phenotype reproduce across genetic tools, clones and cancer types, and is it reversed by add-back?
figureAudit.intervention: Multiple sgRNAs and shRNAs, independent POR-null clones across lineages, and POR re-expression.
figureAudit.readout: Sensitivity to ML210 and RSL3.
figureAudit.answer: POR loss lowers sensitivity in every tested arrangement and re-expression restores it.
figureAudit.boundary: Reproducibility across clones does not distinguish a direct enzymatic role from a change in overall cellular redox state.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Does POR act by altering drug processing or GPX4 engagement rather than by driving lipid chemistry?
figureAudit.intervention: Alkyne ML210 and RSL3 target-engagement labelling, and combined genetic deletion of GPX4 and POR.
figureAudit.readout: GPX4 capture by the covalent probes, and death kinetics after ferrostatin-1 withdrawal.
figureAudit.answer: POR loss does not reduce probe capture of GPX4, and deleting POR still delays death after ferrostatin-1 withdrawal in GPX4-deleted cells.
figureAudit.boundary: The double knockout only delays rather than abolishes death; two drug-processing explanations are excluded but no direct electron acceptor is identified.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Does POR serve only covalent GPX4 inhibitors, or is it a shared amplifier across ferroptosis initiation mechanisms?
figureAudit.intervention: POR deletion or knockdown combined with erastin, FIN56, buthionine sulfoximine and FINO2.
figureAudit.readout: Cell death across inducers.
figureAudit.answer: POR loss reduces death under all four, placing its action downstream of where these initiators converge on lipid oxidation.
figureAudit.boundary: Shared protection can still reflect a global redox shift, and only a limited set of lines and dose windows was tested, so no claim that all ferroptosis depends on POR is supported.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Does POR change the polyunsaturated phospholipid substrate pool, or the peroxidation reaction that follows it?
figureAudit.intervention: Systematic lipidomic profiling of POR-null versus wild-type cells.
figureAudit.readout: Global lipidome composition, C11-BODIPY, malondialdehyde and LC-MS detection of oxidised arachidonoyl and adrenoyl phosphatidylethanolamines.
figureAudit.answer: The global lipidome is largely unchanged while all three oxidation readouts fall, which localises POR to the peroxidation step.
figureAudit.boundary: The POR-CYP model in this figure remains the authors' interpretation; no CYP partner, direct substrate or in vivo flux is identified, and the failure to detect oxidised phosphatidylcholine is method- and model-limited.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

The lipid-peroxidation conclusion rests on four readouts in Fig. 5: BODIPY-C11 flow cytometry, BODIPY-C11 imaging, a malondialdehyde kit assay and species-resolved redox-lipidomics of hydroperoxy- and hydroxy-PUFA-PE, against an unchanged global lipidome in Fig. 5a. title: Plasticity of ether lipids promotes ferroptosis susceptibility and evasion
theme: PUFA ether-lipid plasticity
conditionVector: OVCAR-8 and 786-O carcinoma lines, differentiated SH-SY5Y neuron-like cells and iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes, plus engineered GPX4-null xenografts in mice; GPX4 is removed pharmacologically or genetically; readouts are genome-wide CRISPR suppressor screens, lipidomic profiling of ether phospholipids and tumour regrowth over weeks.

sixtySecond.story: Susceptibility to ferroptosis is not fixed: it can pre-exist in a lineage or be acquired during a change of cell state, and the lipid basis for that plasticity was unclear.
sixtySecond.advance: Peroxisomes emerge from unbiased screens as contributors to ferroptosis because they supply polyunsaturated ether phospholipids, and tumours that survive GPX4 loss in vivo do so by lowering that lipid pool rather than by restoring GPX4.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Convergent loss of PEX3, PEX10, PEX12, FAR1, AGPS and AGPAT3, rescue by sgRNA-resistant cDNA, matched lipidomics, and late-recurring xenografts that remain GPX4-null while losing ether lipids.
sixtySecond.scope: The in vivo arm is an engineered GPX4-null xenograft under strong selection, and the normal-cell arm uses neuron-like cells and iPSC cardiomyocytes rather than adult tissue.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: Which individual oxidised ether-phospholipid species actually execute or propagate death, and do patient tumours evade therapy by the same AGPS route without engineered GPX4 loss?

figureAudit.question: Can an unbiased screen place peroxisomes as a pro-death node after GPX4 inhibition?
figureAudit.intervention: Genome-wide CRISPR suppressor screens in OVCAR-8 and 786-O, followed by targeted knockout and add-back of an sgRNA-resistant cDNA.
figureAudit.readout: Enrichment of peroxisome biogenesis genes and resulting sensitivity.
figureAudit.answer: PEX3, PEX10 and PEX12 together with ether-lipid synthesis enzymes recur as suppressor hits, knockout lowers sensitivity, and add-back restores it.
figureAudit.boundary: The screen reports dependence of two cultured carcinoma lines under pharmacological GPX4 inhibition; it cannot alone show that all inducers or normal tissues depend on peroxisomes.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Is the peroxisomal contribution mediated by polyunsaturated ether-phospholipid synthesis rather than by general peroxisomal ROS metabolism?
figureAudit.intervention: Loss of PEX genes, FAR1, AGPS or AGPAT3, with add-back and defined lipid delivery.
figureAudit.readout: Lipidomic quantification of ether phospholipids and ferroptosis sensitivity.
figureAudit.answer: All of these losses lower polyunsaturated ether phospholipids and confer resistance, and the rescue experiments converge on the sn-2 polyunsaturated chain rather than the ether bond itself.
figureAudit.boundary: Lipidomics supports a change in lipid class; whether specific ether-phospholipid species carry the lethal oxidation in situ needs oxidised-species and flux evidence.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Do these lipids constitute a real constraint in vivo, and can tumours escape by remodelling them?
figureAudit.intervention: Combined disruption of GPX4 and the ether-lipid pathway in xenografts, and analysis of late-recurring tumours.
figureAudit.readout: Tumour growth, GPX4 status, ether-lipid content and AGPS and TMEM189 expression.
figureAudit.answer: Disrupting the ether-lipid pathway restores growth of GPX4-null xenografts, and late recurrences remain GPX4-null while losing ether lipids, AGPS and TMEM189; AGPS loss alone is sufficient for resistance.
figureAudit.boundary: This is a heavily selected engineered GPX4-null system; recurrence here does not mean patient tumours adapt the same way to clinical GPX4 inhibitors.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Do differentiated, non-transformed cell states raise ether lipids and ferroptosis susceptibility together?
figureAudit.intervention: Neuron-like differentiation of SH-SY5Y cells, iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes, and PEX3 or AGPS knockdown in the cardiomyocytes.
figureAudit.readout: Ether-lipid content and sensitivity to GPX4 inhibition.
figureAudit.answer: Both differentiated states raise polyunsaturated ether lipids and become more sensitive, and knocking down the pathway partially protects cardiomyocytes.
figureAudit.boundary: Neuron-like cells and iPSC cardiomyocytes are not adult brain or heart, some experiments have low biological replication, and no disease tissue injury is shown to run through this route.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: FSP1 is a glutathione-independent ferroptosis suppressor
theme: FSP1-CoQ defence
conditionVector: Human cancer cell lines with GPX4 deleted or inhibited by RSL3 and related inducers, complemented by expression cloning and purified-protein and liposome co-oxidation assays; readouts are survival, lipid-radical probes, oxidised phospholipids and NAD(P)H consumption over acute windows.

sixtySecond.story: Before this work GPX4 and radical-trapping antioxidants were the only recognised controls on ferroptosis, which left the resistance of many cell types unexplained.
sixtySecond.advance: An expression-cloning screen identifies AIFM2, renamed FSP1, as a myristoylation-dependent, membrane-localised NAD(P)H-ubiquinone reductase that suppresses ferroptosis in parallel with GPX4 by regenerating the radical-trapping form of coenzyme Q.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Complementation of GPX4 loss, loss of protection by the non-myristoylatable G2A mutant, weakened protection on COQ2 deletion, and reconstitution with purified FSP1 in a liposome co-oxidation assay.
sixtySecond.scope: A complementation screen preferentially finds bypass capacity reachable by overexpression, so it does not establish that cells at physiological expression depend on FSP1; iFSP1 is a first-generation human-cell probe with a known species limitation.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: How is flux actually divided between FSP1, the coenzyme Q pools and GPX4 in different membrane compartments, and which tumours form a treatable FSP1 dependence in vivo?

figureAudit.question: Is there a gene that can sustain survival independently when GPX4 is deleted or inhibited?
figureAudit.intervention: Full-length cDNA expression cloning in human cancer cells lacking GPX4, followed by gain and loss of AIFM2.
figureAudit.readout: Survival under GPX4 deletion and several classes of ferroptosis inducer.
figureAudit.answer: AIFM2 is recovered repeatedly; its overexpression confers resistance and its loss increases sensitivity.
figureAudit.boundary: This screen design finds bypass capacity achievable by high expression, so it cannot alone establish a requirement in normal tissue at endogenous levels.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does the anti-ferroptotic activity of FSP1 depend on N-terminal myristoylation and membrane localisation?
figureAudit.intervention: The G2A substitution and pharmacological inhibition of myristoylation.
figureAudit.readout: Subcellular localisation and protection against RSL3.
figureAudit.answer: Both changes move FSP1 from membranes to a diffuse distribution and abolish protection.
figureAudit.boundary: Colocalisation reports proximity to membrane systems rather than a single executing membrane, and G2A may also alter local conformation or protein interactions, which this work does not exclude structurally.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: What chemistry does FSP1 use to suppress phospholipid peroxidation?
figureAudit.intervention: COQ2 deletion, and reconstitution with purified FSP1, NAD(P)H, coenzyme Q and liposomes.
figureAudit.readout: Lipid radicals and oxidised phospholipids in cells; NAD(P)H consumption, coenzyme Q reduction and liposome co-oxidation in vitro.
figureAudit.answer: FSP1 lowers lipid radicals and oxidised phospholipids, COQ2 loss weakens protection, and purified FSP1 reduces coenzyme Q while suppressing liposome co-oxidation.
figureAudit.boundary: Reconstitution shows chemical feasibility; the relative flux through different quinone pools, electron donors and membrane compartments inside a cell is not quantified in situ, and probe signals do not cover all oxidised phospholipid species.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does inhibiting FSP1 expose a combinable vulnerability in tumour cells?
figureAudit.intervention: The small molecule iFSP1 in cells with and without FSP1, combined with ferroptosis inducers.
figureAudit.readout: Inducer potency and its dependence on FSP1 status, and correlation of sensitivity with FSP1 expression across lines.
figureAudit.answer: iFSP1 selectively increases inducer potency in an FSP1-dependent manner, and line sensitivity correlates with FSP1 expression.
figureAudit.boundary: iFSP1 is a first-generation human-cell probe that does not act on mouse FSP1; a correlation across lines is not a patient predictive marker, and no systemic dosing window in animals is shown.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: A non-canonical vitamin K cycle is a potent ferroptosis suppressor
theme: FSP1 and vitamin K
conditionVector: Cells with GPX4 deleted or inhibited, hepatocyte-specific Gpx4 knockout mice, liver and kidney ischaemia-reperfusion with pre-injury dosing, and warfarin-overdose mice; vitamin K forms are given at nutritional excess; readouts are death, oxidised phosphatidylethanolamines, FENIX liposome kinetics and clotting parameters.

sixtySecond.story: Vitamin K was known as a clotting cofactor, and the warfarin-resistant enzyme that reduces it in the canonical cycle had been unidentified for decades.
sixtySecond.advance: FSP1 reduces vitamin K to its hydroquinone, which acts as a potent membrane radical-trapping antioxidant, giving a non-canonical vitamin K cycle that suppresses ferroptosis and simultaneously explains warfarin-resistant vitamin K reduction.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Recombinant FSP1 generates the hydroquinone and confers dose-dependent radical-trapping activity in liposomes; FSP1 loss or iFSP1 shifts cellular protection, the membrane-defective G2A mutant cannot restore it, and Fsp1-null mice cannot be rescued from warfarin overdose by high-dose vitamin K.
sixtySecond.scope: The animal protection uses supranutritional dosing given before injury, and the in vivo anti-ferroptotic arm was not tested on an Fsp1-null background, so this is pharmacology rather than a dietary claim.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: How do coenzyme Q and the vitamin K forms actually compete for FSP1 in different tissues, and can the membrane antioxidant benefit be separated from clotting risk and drug interaction?

figureAudit.question: Can vitamin K act as an anti-ferroptotic class independent of vitamin E, and does it protect tissue models?
figureAudit.intervention: Phylloquinone, menaquinone-4 and menadione in cells with genetic or pharmacological GPX4 loss; menaquinone-4 in hepatocyte Gpx4-knockout mice and before liver and kidney ischaemia-reperfusion.
figureAudit.readout: Cell death, oxidised phosphatidylethanolamines, survival and organ injury markers.
figureAudit.answer: All three forms suppress death and oxidised phosphatidylethanolamines, menaquinone-4 delays death in the knockout mice, and pre-injury dosing lowers ischaemia-reperfusion injury.
figureAudit.boundary: The animals received supranutritional menaquinone-4 before injury, and the in vivo protection was not tested on an Fsp1-null background, so this cannot be written as an effect of ordinary dietary vitamin K.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Can FSP1 reduce vitamin K directly to the hydroquinone that does the radical trapping?
figureAudit.intervention: Recombinant FSP1 with NADH and three vitamin K forms, and the FENIX liposome assay.
figureAudit.readout: NADH consumption, hydroquinone formation, and dose-dependent inhibition of lipid autoxidation.
figureAudit.answer: FSP1 consumes NADH and generates menaquinone-4 hydroquinone, and all three forms acquire dose-dependent radical-trapping activity whose rate and duration track the side chain.
figureAudit.boundary: Reconstitution establishes chemical feasibility and relative kinetics; it does not measure competition between coenzyme Q and vitamin K inside cellular membrane compartments.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does cellular protection by vitamin K depend on FSP1 and its membrane localisation at the lower end of the dose range?
figureAudit.intervention: FSP1 knockout, iFSP1, add-back of wild-type FSP1 versus the G2A membrane-binding mutant, and warfarin co-treatment.
figureAudit.readout: Dose-response of phylloquinone and menaquinone-4 protection.
figureAudit.answer: FSP1 loss or inhibition shifts the protection curve to the right, wild-type but not G2A add-back restores it, and warfarin does not block the anti-ferroptotic effect.
figureAudit.boundary: High concentrations still protect FSP1-deficient cells, which implies NQO1, thioredoxin reductase or non-enzymatic reduction as alternative routes, and iFSP1 tool-compound limitations remain.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Is FSP1 also the unidentified warfarin-resistant vitamin K reductase of classical clotting biology?
figureAudit.intervention: FSP1 loss in HepG2 cells and mice, and warfarin overdose rescued with high-dose menaquinone-4.
figureAudit.readout: Menaquinone-4 to epoxide turnover, clotting parameters and survival.
figureAudit.answer: FSP1 loss reduces turnover, and Fsp1-null mice cannot be corrected normally by high-dose menaquinone-4 while one intact allele is sufficient for rescue.
figureAudit.boundary: This establishes a clotting and detoxification physiology, not new in vivo ferroptosis evidence, and the warfarin setting does not report vitamin K flux through FSP1 in other tissues.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: Oxidized arachidonic and adrenic PEs navigate cells to ferroptosis
theme: redox lipidomics of oxidised PE
conditionVector: Cultured cells with RSL3 or genetic Gpx4 loss, with and without exogenous arachidonic acid loading, plus inducible Gpx4-knockout mouse kidney; readouts are LiperFluo imaging, quantitative redox lipidomics of about 350 phospholipids and their oxidation products, deuterated fatty-acid tracing and defined oxidised-lipid supplementation over acute windows.

sixtySecond.story: Ferroptosis was known to involve lipid peroxidation, but which oxidation products act as the proximate death signal was unresolved and often treated as undifferentiated lipid ROS.
sixtySecond.advance: Quantitative redox lipidomics narrows the lethal signal to a small set of doubly and triply oxygenated phosphatidylethanolamines carrying arachidonoyl or adrenoyl chains, whose formation requires ACSL4 and LPCAT3 esterification.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Multi-filter prioritisation across roughly 350 phospholipids, recurrence of the same species in genetically Gpx4-deficient cells and mouse kidney, deuterated arachidonate tracing into the oxidised species, and restoration of death by supplying the preformed esterified hydroperoxide.
sixtySecond.scope: Most work uses exogenous arachidonic acid loading and acute RSL3 exposure; supplying about 0.9 micromolar of the esterified hydroperoxide alone did not reproduce comparable death and acted mainly as a sensitiser.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: Which of these species are directly sufficient at physiological abundance rather than process markers, and how are enzymatic and non-enzymatic oxidation routes divided across tissues?

figureAudit.question: Can bulk lipid-peroxidation fluorescence distinguish the esterified signal that tracks death?
figureAudit.intervention: RSL3 treatment of wild-type and ACSL4-deficient cells.
figureAudit.readout: LiperFluo localisation and intensity alongside free and esterified hydroperoxide levels and death.
figureAudit.answer: The signal concentrates in endoplasmic-reticulum-associated compartments, and ACSL4-deficient cells accumulate more free polyunsaturated hydroperoxide yet die less, so bulk intensity does not track lethality.
figureAudit.boundary: LiperFluo reduces hydroperoxides and reports location only by proxy, so the correlation cannot by itself establish the endoplasmic reticulum as the sole endogenous origin.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Does entry of arachidonate and adrenate into membrane phospholipids require ACSL4 and LPCAT3 and set sensitivity?
figureAudit.intervention: Arachidonic acid supplementation, ACSL4 deletion and Lpcat3 knockdown by Cre-inducible short hairpin RNA.
figureAudit.readout: Acyl-CoA and phospholipid composition and RSL3-induced death.
figureAudit.answer: Supplementation raises the corresponding acyl-CoAs and phospholipids and increases death, while loss of either enzyme lowers esterification and confers resistance.
figureAudit.boundary: This is membrane remodelling under exogenous loading and acute inhibition, which does not represent tissue fatty-acid supply, carrier binding or long-term homeostasis.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

correction.correctedBy: Claude Code round-4 primary-source pass (implementer, not an independent reviewer)
correction.was: Arachidonic acid supplementation, ACSL4 deletion and LPCAT3 inhibition.
correction.why: The Methods section "Lpcat3 Knock-down" describes a pSico Cre-lox short-hairpin system with knockdown confirmed by western blot, not pharmacological inhibition. Acyl-CoA composition, also attributed here previously, is measured in Fig. 1f.

figureAudit.question: Can a specific set of oxidised species be separated from the large number of oxidation products?
figureAudit.intervention: Quantitative redox lipidomic screening of about 350 phospholipids and their oxidation products through successive filters.
figureAudit.readout: Death-associated increase, ACSL4 dependence and recurrence under in vivo GPX4 loss.
figureAudit.answer: Four molecular species in a single phospholipid class - doubly and triply oxygenated arachidonoyl- and adrenoyl-phosphatidylethanolamine - satisfy all four filters simultaneously, narrowing 110 candidates through 44, 17 and 8.
figureAudit.boundary: This prioritises candidates from correlated changes; passing multiple filters does not establish necessity, sufficiency or uniqueness for any individual species.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

correction.correctedBy: Claude Code round-4 primary-source pass (implementer, not an independent reviewer)
correction.was: Four classes of doubly and triply oxygenated arachidonoyl- and adrenoyl-phosphatidylethanolamine satisfy all filters simultaneously.
correction.why: The source says "Only four molecular species of phospholipids in only one class of phospholipids". Calling them four classes overstates the breadth of the result by a factor of four.

figureAudit.question: Do the candidate species recur in genetically GPX4-deficient cells and mouse kidney?
figureAudit.intervention: Gpx4-deficient cells and inducible knockout mice, with and without liproxstatin-1.
figureAudit.readout: Targeted quantification of the same oxidised phosphatidylethanolamines.
figureAudit.answer: The same species accumulate in both settings and liproxstatin-1 lowers them.
figureAudit.boundary: The tissue endpoint follows acute genetic deletion, so accumulation serves as a process marker without showing that each species executes kidney injury.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Does arachidonate reach the lethal oxidised species along an ACSL4-dependent route?
figureAudit.intervention: Deuterated arachidonic acid tracing with and without ACSL4.
figureAudit.readout: Isotope flow into elongated adrenate and into doubly and triply oxygenated diacyl species.
figureAudit.answer: The label is elongated and enters the oxidised diacyl species, and ACSL4 loss reduces that flow, while alkyl and alkenyl ether species do not change in the same way.
figureAudit.boundary: Tracing supports the precursor route but rests on exogenous isotope loading and targeted detection, so absolute flux through all endogenous pools is not measured.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

figureAudit.question: Can a preformed esterified arachidonate hydroperoxide restore the ferroptotic response in ACSL4-deficient cells?
figureAudit.intervention: Supplying the esterified hydroperoxide, free hydroperoxide or the acyl-CoA form; lipoxygenase inhibition and in vitro 15-lipoxygenase reactions.
figureAudit.readout: RSL3-induced death and formation of the corresponding oxidised phosphatidylethanolamines.
figureAudit.answer: Only the esterified hydroperoxide increases death in ACSL4-deficient cells, lipoxygenase inhibitors protect, and 15-lipoxygenase can generate the corresponding species in vitro.
figureAudit.boundary: About 0.9 micromolar of the esterified hydroperoxide alone did not cause comparable death and acted mainly as a sensitiser under RSL3, and pharmacological lipoxygenase results cannot be extended to all models as the sole oxidation source.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure caption read in full in the PMC author manuscript on 2026-07-24; the rendered figure image and the supplementary material were not opened.

title: Activation of lysosomal iron triggers ferroptosis in cancer
theme: lysosomal iron and membrane damage
conditionVector: Cancer cell lines, primary sarcoma and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma cells, organoids and drug-tolerant persister states, plus a 4T1 lymph-node metastasis model with intranodal dosing; inducers are RSL3 or the designed activator fentomycin-1; readouts are clickable-probe localisation, iron probes, LiperFluo and C11-BODIPY at one to four hours and proteomics at 24 to 48 hours.

sixtySecond.story: Where in the cell iron actually drives membrane lipid oxidation determines what a ferroptosis drug has to reach, and genetic screens do not resolve that geography.
sixtySecond.advance: Liproxstatin-1 is shown to act in part by inactivating lysosomal iron, RSL3-initiated oxidation is detected first in lysosomes, and a purpose-built activator of lysosomal iron degrades phospholipids and kills iron-rich CD44-high tumour cells including drug-tolerant persisters.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: A clickable liproxstatin-1 derivative that enriches in lysosomes and protects in a Gpx4-deficient kidney model, iron-binding measured by NMR and cyclic voltammetry, and a designed bifunctional molecule that produces oxidised and cleaved phospholipids and rescuable death.
sixtySecond.scope: Localisation is shown with modified probe derivatives, the ordering experiments use lysosomal pH drugs and few time points, and the in vivo arm is local intranodal dosing rather than systemic exposure.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: Can the lysosome-to-endoplasmic-reticulum ordering be confirmed with non-perturbing molecular-species imaging, and does fentomycin-1 have a systemic pharmacokinetic and toxicity window?

figureAudit.question: Does liproxstatin-1 act not only by trapping radicals but also by localising to lysosomes and inactivating reactive iron?
figureAudit.intervention: A clickable liproxstatin-1 derivative, a Gpx4-deficient kidney injury model, and direct iron-binding chemistry.
figureAudit.readout: Subcellular enrichment, tissue protection, NMR and cyclic voltammetry, and iron-probe reactivity.
figureAudit.answer: The derivative enriches in lysosomes and protects in vivo, and liproxstatin-1 binds iron and lowers its redox reactivity.
figureAudit.boundary: The labelled derivative is a surrogate, so its localisation does not show that all activity of the unmodified drug occurs in lysosomes, and iron inactivation is an additional rather than a proven sole mechanism.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure, Extended Data 2 and 3, and legends as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Under standard GPX4 inhibition, do lysosomal iron and lipid oxidation signals precede endoplasmic-reticulum signals?
figureAudit.intervention: Raising lysosomal pH, then RSL3 treatment with a one-hour and four-hour comparison.
figureAudit.readout: Available iron, C11-BODIPY and LiperFluo colocalisation with lysosomal and endoplasmic-reticulum markers.
figureAudit.answer: Raising lysosomal pH lowers available iron and weakens the C11 signal; at one hour the probes overlap mainly with lysosomes and the endoplasmic-reticulum signal increases by four hours.
figureAudit.boundary: Lysosomal pH drugs also perturb autophagy, trafficking and metabolism, the probes participate in radical and iron chemistry, and few time points cannot establish a universal origin across inducers and cell types.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure, Extended Data 2 and 3, and legends as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Can a chemical trigger be designed that activates iron only in acidic lysosomes and oxidises membrane phospholipids?
figureAudit.intervention: Fentomycin-1, combining a membrane-avid marmycin scaffold with an iron-activating ligand, applied to acidic liposomes and to cells.
figureAudit.readout: Iron oxidation chemistry, oxidised and cleaved phospholipids, rescuable death, and proteomics at 24 to 48 hours.
figureAudit.answer: Fentomycin-1 promotes iron oxidation chemistry in acidic compartments, raises oxidised and cleaved phospholipids, and induces ferroptosis that inhibitors rescue.
figureAudit.boundary: This is a deliberately targeted bifunctional reagent, so it shows that lysosomal chemistry is sufficient to initiate the process rather than that RSL3 necessarily uses the same route; the late proteomics reports adaptation, not the initiating mechanism.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Are CD44-high, iron-rich resistant tumour states sensitive to this trigger, and does a window exist in vivo?
figureAudit.intervention: Patient sarcoma and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma material, primary cells, organoids and drug-tolerant persister states; intranodal dosing in a 4T1 lymph-node metastasis model.
figureAudit.readout: Total and lysosomal iron reactivity, killing, tumour burden and oxidised lipids.
figureAudit.answer: CD44-high subsets show higher iron reactivity, all the ex vivo states are sensitive to fentomycin-1, and local dosing lowers tumour burden while raising oxidised lipids.
figureAudit.boundary: Technical replicates in patient material must be separated from independent patients, ex vivo killing is not clinical response, and intranodal dosing does not represent systemic exposure, whole-body toxicity or a manufacturable window.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: Ferroptosis spreads to neighboring cells via plasma membrane contacts
theme: propagation between cells
conditionVector: Cultured monolayers at defined confluency carrying an optogenetic GPX4 degradation system, triggered in single cells or small groups by patterned blue light; plus protein-free supported bilayers and contacting giant vesicles; readouts are C11-BODIPY oxidation, death timing versus distance, and mass spectrometry of oxidised lipids over minutes to hours.

sixtySecond.story: Necrosis spreads through diseased tissue, but whether ferroptosis itself passes from cell to cell, and by what route, had no controlled test because inducers are normally added to the whole dish.
sixtySecond.advance: Light-driven degradation of GPX4 in a chosen cell shows that lipid peroxidation and death spread to immediate neighbours in a distance-dependent way that requires alpha-catenin-dependent contacts and extracellular iron, and the transfer can be reconstituted between pure contacting bilayers.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Single-cell optogenetic triggering with an apoptotic optogenetic control, distance-dependent secondary death blocked by ferrostatin-1 added after activation, failure of conditioned medium to reproduce it, alpha-catenin loss and add-back, and iron-dependent transfer between contacting vesicles.
sixtySecond.scope: Everything is measured in two-dimensional culture geometry with probe-based oxidation readouts; the vesicle experiment establishes physicochemical sufficiency in a minimal system, not the dominant mechanism in tissue.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: What actually crosses the interface, and how much does contact transfer contribute relative to reactive-oxygen waves and paracrine signals in solid tumour, ischaemic or neural tissue?

figureAudit.question: Can GPX4 loss and ferroptosis be triggered with spatial and temporal control rather than by treating the whole dish?
figureAudit.intervention: An optogenetic GPX4 degradation construct activated by blue light.
figureAudit.readout: GPX4 levels, lipid oxidation, ferrostatin-1-sensitive death and mass spectrometry of oxidised lipids.
figureAudit.answer: Illumination rapidly degrades GPX4 and produces ferrostatin-1-sensitive lipid oxidation and death, with oxidised lipids detected by mass spectrometry.
figureAudit.boundary: Illumination and the degradation system introduce transient background oxidation, and the mass-spectrometry arm has low biological replication, so bulk oxidised-lipid increase cannot identify the transferred molecule.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does single-cell activation restrict initiation to the target cell and leave neighbours observable?
figureAudit.intervention: Digital micromirror patterning of light onto single cells or small groups.
figureAudit.readout: Time-resolved GPX4 degradation and C11-BODIPY oxidation in target and non-illuminated cells.
figureAudit.answer: Target cells degrade GPX4 and oxidise first while non-illuminated cells initially remain intact.
figureAudit.boundary: C11-BODIPY is a proxy readout, the light pattern and cell migration require continuous correction, and selective triggering does not identify the propagating species.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Do non-illuminated neighbours subsequently die in a ferroptosis-dependent way?
figureAudit.intervention: Distance analysis around triggered cells, an apoptotic optogenetic control, and ferrostatin-1 added after activation.
figureAudit.readout: Delayed C11 oxidation and death probability as a function of distance.
figureAudit.answer: Immediate neighbours oxidise and die with a delay, probability falls with distance, the apoptotic control does not reproduce the pattern, and late ferrostatin-1 still blocks spreading.
figureAudit.boundary: Distance dependence does not separate membrane contact from a local reactive-oxygen wave or a very short-lived paracrine factor, and dish geometry differs from three-dimensional tissue.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does spreading depend on a stable soluble factor, calcium signalling, or contact and density?
figureAudit.intervention: Conditioned-medium transfer, calcium manipulation, and varying confluency and contact.
figureAudit.readout: Neighbour death.
figureAudit.answer: Conditioned medium does not reproduce spreading and calcium manipulation does not explain it, whereas higher confluency and closer contact strongly increase neighbour death.
figureAudit.boundary: A negative conditioned-medium result only excludes mediators stable and abundant enough to survive collection and transfer; locally generated, labile or contact-requiring mediators remain possible.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Is alpha-catenin, which maintains the adhesive interface, required for spreading?
figureAudit.intervention: Alpha-catenin loss and E-cadherin perturbation.
figureAudit.readout: Neighbour propagation versus death of the directly activated cell.
figureAudit.answer: Alpha-catenin loss strongly reduces propagation while directly activated cells still die, and E-cadherin perturbation gives a more complex and partly opposite result.
figureAudit.boundary: Alpha-catenin also changes mechanotransduction, YAP signalling and anti-ferroptotic protein levels, so protection cannot be read as cutting a single molecular conduit, and the E-cadherin result shows the adhesion network is not one pathway.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Can the requirement be attributed to the contact-organising function of alpha-catenin by genetic add-back?
figureAudit.intervention: Re-expression of wild-type alpha-catenin and of mutants that cannot maintain junctions in the deficient background.
figureAudit.readout: Restoration of neighbour propagation.
figureAudit.answer: Wild-type protein restores propagation while junction-defective mutants restore it poorly, supporting a role for the contact structure.
figureAudit.boundary: Add-back strengthens genetic causality but alpha-catenin retains multiple scaffolding and signalling roles, and no oxidised phospholipid is directly observed crossing the membrane.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Can transfer be reconstituted from contacting lipid bilayers, oxidation and iron alone?
figureAudit.intervention: Artificial membrane bridges and contacting giant vesicles with oxidation initiated in the donor membrane, with and without iron chelation.
figureAudit.readout: Appearance of oxidation in the acceptor membrane.
figureAudit.answer: Oxidation initiated in the donor membrane enters the acceptor membrane and iron chelation changes transfer, showing that membrane proximity, oxidisable lipid and iron are physicochemically sufficient.
figureAudit.boundary: This is sufficiency in a minimal system, not proof of the dominant mechanism in tissue; lipid composition, photosensitiser and contact geometry are all set artificially and no molecular carrier is identified.

title: Ferroptosis inhibition enhances liver and lung graft function

versionEvents.note: This is an internal record change, not a publisher notice. The project archive read this paper as an in-press corrected proof and stored it as corrected. Crossref assigns volume 189, pages 3991-4004.e26 and a June 2026 issue with no correction registered, so the article stage is version of record and the post-publication status is none. An Elsevier corrected proof is a stage in production, not a published correction.

theme: transplantation and ex situ human organs
conditionVector: Cells, ischaemic porcine liver reperfused ex situ with whole blood for six hours, porcine lung ex vivo perfusion for eight hours, five declined human donor lungs perfused as split left and right pairs, and plasma from 116 human liver transplant recipients; FXT-001 is added to the preservation or perfusion circuit; readouts are transaminases, bile production, wet-to-dry ratio, compliance, computed tomography density, cytokines and malondialdehyde.

sixtySecond.story: Ischaemia-reperfusion injury limits how many donor organs can be used, and ferroptosis has been proposed as a driver without a preservation-stage intervention to test it.
sixtySecond.advance: An early, transient window of lipid oxidation is identified in human liver transplants, and FXT-001, which both traps radicals in membranes and binds iron, improves ex situ function of porcine liver and lung grafts and of declined human lungs.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Paired split perfusion of left and right lungs from the same declined human donor, plus porcine liver reperfusion and eight-hour porcine lung perfusion with matched physiological and oxidation readouts.
sixtySecond.scope: No treated organ was transplanted into a recipient; the human lung arm has five donors with one lung per condition and the authors define it as descriptive, and malondialdehyde is a non-specific end product of lipid oxidation.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: Does treatment improve early graft function, rejection and long-term survival after actual transplantation, and which oxidised phospholipid markers predict rescuable organs better than malondialdehyde?

figureAudit.question: Does FXT-001 have the medicinal-chemistry, pharmacokinetic and safety properties needed to move from cells to organ perfusion?
figureAudit.intervention: Characterisation of FXT-001 and generation of the FXT-002 and FXT-003 analogues.
figureAudit.readout: Cellular potency, oral and intravenous pharmacokinetics, tissue distribution and an early ADME and safety panel.
figureAudit.answer: FXT-001 protects cells at nanomolar concentrations and has a workable pharmacokinetic and distribution profile that supports the analogue series.
figureAudit.boundary: This is discovery-stage pharmacology rather than regulated safety assessment; hERG inhibition and safety-panel hits appear at ten micromolar, and total tissue concentration is not free effective concentration.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: How does FXT-001 act on membrane lipid radical propagation and on available iron?
figureAudit.intervention: Fluorescent and clickable analogues for localisation, the FENIX liposome assay, and iron-binding and iron-overload assays.
figureAudit.readout: Membrane partitioning, inhibition of lipid autoxidation, Fe3+ binding, lysosomal and mitochondrial Fe2+.
figureAudit.answer: The compound inserts into several internal membranes, truncates lipid autoxidation, binds Fe3+ and lowers lysosomal and mitochondrial labile iron and iron-overload injury.
figureAudit.boundary: Partitioning of fluorescent or clickable derivatives need not match the parent drug, and simultaneous multi-compartment localisation, radical trapping and iron redistribution prevent assignment of a single target or compartment.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does adding FXT-001 locally protect an ischaemic porcine liver during ex situ whole-blood reperfusion?
figureAudit.intervention: FXT-001 added to the cold preservation solution before six-hour reperfusion.
figureAudit.readout: ALT, AST, LDH, glucose, histological injury, bile production and lipid-oxidation readouts.
figureAudit.answer: Injury markers and tissue damage fall, bile production and metabolic function improve, and lipid-oxidation readouts decrease.
figureAudit.boundary: About five organs per group observed for six hours ex situ, with no implantation, rejection or survival data, so this is preservation proof of concept.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Is there a lipid-oxidation time window in real liver transplant patients that tracks early graft injury?
figureAudit.intervention: Serial plasma sampling in 116 liver transplant recipients.
figureAudit.readout: Relative malondialdehyde over time and its relation to six-hour AST.
figureAudit.answer: Relative malondialdehyde peaks about thirty minutes after reperfusion and early values relate to six-hour AST and to high versus low AST groups.
figureAudit.boundary: Malondialdehyde is a non-specific end product, so the association cannot show that these patients underwent ferroptosis or exclude necrosis, inflammation and surgical exposure as contributors.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does FXT-001 improve oedema, compliance and inflammation during porcine lung ex vivo perfusion?
figureAudit.intervention: FXT-001 during eight-hour porcine lung ex vivo perfusion.
figureAudit.readout: Wet-to-dry ratio, weight gain, computed tomography density, dynamic compliance, IL-6, IL-8 and malondialdehyde.
figureAudit.answer: Oedema measures and computed tomography density improve, dynamic compliance improves, and IL-6, IL-8 and part of the malondialdehyde accumulation fall.
figureAudit.boundary: Small group sizes, limited perfusion time and no subsequent transplantation; the improvement may reflect broad antioxidant, iron-binding or anti-inflammatory effects, and malondialdehyde alone cannot name the death mode.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Within the same human donor, does FXT-001 protect lungs that would otherwise be declined?
figureAudit.intervention: Left and right lungs from five declined donors perfused in parallel with FXT-001 or vehicle.
figureAudit.readout: Wet-to-dry ratio, weight gain, compliance and computed tomography appearance.
figureAudit.answer: The treated side is generally better on all four measures.
figureAudit.boundary: Five donors with one lung per condition and results defined by the authors as descriptive; no organ was transplanted and there are no long-term function, infection or rejection outcomes.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

reviews.sourceVersion: Nature volume 645, page E9 (2025); Publisher Correction published 26 August 2025; publisher HTML retrieved in full 2026-07-24

theme: sex difference in kidney injury
conditionVector: Male and female mouse kidney ischaemia-reperfusion, freshly isolated kidney tubules, ovariectomy, tubule-specific Gpx4, Fsp1 and Esr1 deletion, and small human sample sets; oestradiol derivatives are applied at about ten micromolar in cells and ten milligrams per kilogram before injury; readouts are creatinine and urea, synchronised tubular death imaging, FENIX kinetics and protein expression.

sixtySecond.story: Female kidneys have been known for decades to resist acute kidney injury, and tubular necrosis in this setting propagates as ferroptosis, but the protective chemistry was unidentified.
sixtySecond.advance: Oestradiol acts through two separable routes: hydroxyoestradiol derivatives directly trap lipid radicals and can be regenerated by FSP1, while ESR1 maintains a hydropersulfide defence and suppresses pro-ferroptotic ether-lipid enzymes in female tubules.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Radical-trapping activity measured in reconstituted membranes, protection of male mice by pre-injury dosing, loss of tolerance after ovariectomy, and tubule-specific Esr1 deletion that sensitises female tissue without lowering oestradiol levels.
sixtySecond.scope: Cellular work uses about ten micromolar and animal work uses ten milligrams per kilogram given before injury, which is not hormone replacement; female Fsp1-null mice retained tolerance, so FSP1 does not account for the whole effect.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: What concentration do hydroxyoestradiols actually reach in human kidney, and can a kidney-targeted protective chemistry be used after injury without activating systemic oestrogen receptors?

figureAudit.question: Do female kidneys show reproducible ferroptosis resistance in vivo and in isolated tubules?
figureAudit.intervention: Male and female ischaemia-reperfusion, freshly isolated tubules, ferrostatin-1 or UAMC-3203, and tubular Gpx4 deletion.
figureAudit.readout: Creatinine and urea, synchronised tubular death imaging and phenotype severity.
figureAudit.answer: Female animals and tubules are markedly less affected, the inhibitors protect mainly males, and tubular Gpx4 deletion is also more severe in males.
figureAudit.boundary: Synchronised death imaging cannot separate true cell-to-cell signalling from shared exposure, and a mouse sex difference does not show that human sex differences in acute kidney injury are entirely ferroptotic.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Can oestradiol derivatives truncate lipid radical chains by direct chemistry rather than receptor signalling?
figureAudit.intervention: 2-hydroxy and 4-hydroxy oestradiol in the FENIX assay, in several cell types and in male primary tubules; pre-injury dosing in males and ovariectomy in females.
figureAudit.readout: Radical-trapping kinetics, cell and tubule death, and injury markers.
figureAudit.answer: Both derivatives show radical-trapping activity, 2-hydroxyoestradiol protects cells and male tubules, pre-injury dosing lowers injury in males, and ovariectomy increases injury in females.
figureAudit.boundary: Ten micromolar in cells and ten milligrams per kilogram given before injury is not physiological hormone replacement, FENIX is reconstituted membrane chemistry, and pre-treatment cannot answer whether a post-injury window or systemic hormone risk is acceptable.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Are the oxidised hydroxyoestradiols recycled by FSP1?
figureAudit.intervention: Recombinant FSP1 with NADPH in FENIX, AIFM2-deficient cells, and female Fsp1-deficient tubules and mice.
figureAudit.readout: Radical-trapping capacity, rescue dose requirement and injury.
figureAudit.answer: FSP1 and NADPH increase the radical-trapping capacity of hydroxyoestradiol and FSP1-deficient cells need more of it, but female Fsp1-deficient tubules and mice do not lose tolerance completely.
figureAudit.boundary: The retained in vivo phenotype indicates peroxiredoxin, thioredoxin or other compensation, so the sex difference cannot be assigned entirely to FSP1 and hydroxyoestradiol.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does ESR1 provide a genomic protective layer separate from acute radical-trapping chemistry?
figureAudit.intervention: Tubule-specific Esr1 deletion in female mice, with ischaemia-reperfusion and ferrostatin-1.
figureAudit.readout: Tubular sensitivity, injury markers and oestradiol and hydroxyoestradiol levels.
figureAudit.answer: Esr1 deletion sensitises female tubules and increases injury even though hormone levels are unchanged, supporting a transcriptional mechanism.
figureAudit.boundary: ESR1 affects broad transcription and renal physiology, so this does not show that all of the injury difference is ferroptotic; ferrostatin-1 rescue supports involvement without excluding other death programmes.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Which ESR1-dependent metabolic branches set the female tubular ferroptosis threshold?
figureAudit.intervention: Comparison of female, male and ESR1-deficient tubules, plus supplementation with an oxidised persulfide donor and small human sample sets.
figureAudit.readout: CSE, ETHE1 and SQR levels, glutathione persulfide, ether-lipid enzymes and rescue of cells and male tubules.
figureAudit.answer: Female and ESR1-competent tissue has higher CSE, lower ETHE1 and SQR, more glutathione persulfide and suppressed AGPS, FAR1 and ACSL4, and the persulfide donor rescues cells and male tubules.
figureAudit.boundary: Several correlated branches are presented in parallel without quantifying their causal weight, and the human sets have about four samples per group with protein expression only, so post-menopausal kidney injury is not shown to be driven by ether-lipid ferroptosis.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: DHODH-mediated ferroptosis defence is a targetable vulnerability in cancer

versionEvents.note: Read in full on 2026-07-24. The notice changes one sentence of the Acknowledgements, adding "and University of Texas PDX Development and Trial Center grant U54CA224065" to the list of sources supporting PDX generation and annotation, and states that the original Article has been corrected online. It touches funding attribution only; no method, figure, dataset or conclusion is mentioned.

reviews.checkedBy: Claude Code round-4 primary-source pass (implementer, not an independent reviewer)
reviews.sourceVersion: Nature volume 596, page E13 (2021); Author Correction published 02 August 2021; publisher HTML retrieved in full 2026-07-24
reviews.boundary: Complete for the notice. It is a funding-attribution change and carries no information about the disputed DHODH target attribution, which is the subject of a separate Matters Arising and Reply that were not opened here.

versionEvents.note: Published as 'DHODH inhibitors sensitize to ferroptosis by FSP1 inhibition'. It argues that the brequinar concentrations used also inhibit FSP1, so sensitisation cannot be attributed to DHODH alone. This is a formal scientific dispute, not a correction or a retraction.
The authors' reply to the Matters Arising, published on the same date. Both sides remain visible in the record and neither is presented here as the settled outcome.

theme: contested mitochondrial defence
conditionVector: Cancer cell lines stratified by GPX4 expression, with RSL3 or ML162 and with brequinar often at 500 micromolar, uridine supplied to remove the pyrimidine deficit, plus xenografts and patient-derived xenografts dosed with brequinar at 30 milligrams per kilogram every three days and sulfasalazine at 100 milligrams per kilogram daily in immunodeficient mice; readouts are mitochondrial C11-BODIPY, coenzyme Q redox ratio, 4-hydroxynonenal and tumour growth.

sixtySecond.story: GPX4 and FSP1 were the recognised ferroptosis defences, and whether mitochondria carry an independent one was unresolved.
sixtySecond.advance: The work proposes that dihydroorotate dehydrogenase reduces coenzyme Q in the inner mitochondrial membrane in parallel with mitochondrial GPX4, and that inhibiting it exposes GPX4-low tumours; a later Matters Arising argues the pharmacology is confounded by FSP1 inhibition at the concentrations used.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Acute depletion of carbamoyl-aspartate after GPX4 inhibition, opposite effects of dihydroorotate and orotate, a genetic interaction between DHODH loss and GPX4 knockdown maintained under uridine supplementation, and rescue by catalytically and mitochondrially competent DHODH only.
sixtySecond.scope: Much of the key pharmacology uses 500 micromolar brequinar, which the 2023 Matters Arising shows also inhibits FSP1; the in vivo arm is immunodeficient xenografts with combination dosing.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: With a DHODH inhibitor that does not inhibit FSP1, which genetic backgrounds still show sensitisation, and can GPX4-low become a reproducible predictive marker rather than a post-hoc stratification?

figureAudit.question: Do metabolic changes after GPX4 inhibition point to DHODH as a defence?
figureAudit.intervention: RSL3 or ML162 treatment, dihydroorotate or orotate supplementation, and brequinar in GPX4-low NCI-H226 cells.
figureAudit.readout: Carbamoyl-aspartate and uridine levels, lipid oxidation and liproxstatin-1-rescuable death.
figureAudit.answer: GPX4 inhibition rapidly depletes carbamoyl-aspartate and raises uridine, the substrate and product have opposite effects on sensitivity, and brequinar induces rescuable death in GPX4-low cells.
figureAudit.boundary: Key pharmacology uses 500 micromolar brequinar, far above what is needed to inhibit DHODH, and the 2023 Matters Arising shows this concentration also inhibits FSP1, so the sensitisation in this figure cannot be attributed to DHODH alone.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure, Extended Data 1 to 9 and the 2023 Matters Arising and Reply as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does genetic loss of DHODH produce an independent phenotype when GPX4 is low?
figureAudit.intervention: DHODH knockout in HT1080 and GPX4-low NCI-H226 with uridine supplementation, combined with GPX4 knockdown.
figureAudit.readout: Lipid oxidation, liproxstatin-1-rescuable death and genetic interaction.
figureAudit.answer: DHODH knockout increases RSL3 sensitivity and still produces rescuable death when uridine maintains pyrimidine supply, and it interacts genetically with GPX4 knockdown.
figureAudit.boundary: Independent replication reports a smaller and context-dependent genetic phenotype; uridine removes the main pyrimidine deficit but does not prove the residual effect comes only from mitochondrial ubiquinol, and knockout magnitude does not predict a drug window.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure, Extended Data 1 to 9 and the 2023 Matters Arising and Reply as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Is the protection localised to the mitochondrial coenzyme Q pool and parallel to mitochondrial GPX4?
figureAudit.intervention: Add-back of wild-type versus catalytically dead or mislocalised DHODH, mitochondrial GPX4 overexpression, mitoTEMPO and mitoquinol.
figureAudit.readout: Mitochondrial C11-BODIPY, coenzyme Q to ubiquinol ratio and death.
figureAudit.answer: Only competent DHODH rescues, mitochondrial antioxidants protect against the combined stress, and DHODH inhibition raises the oxidised coenzyme Q fraction and mitochondrial lipid oxidation.
figureAudit.boundary: Forced localisation and isoform overexpression do not equal endogenous flux, mitochondrial C11 is a proxy probe, extracted coenzyme Q redox ratios are prone to oxidation artefact, and no rate of oxidised-phospholipid clearance by DHODH is measured in living cells.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure, Extended Data 1 to 9 and the 2023 Matters Arising and Reply as recorded in the project audit archive.

figureAudit.question: Does DHODH inhibition suppress tumours selectively by GPX4 status through ferroptosis?
figureAudit.intervention: Brequinar at 30 milligrams per kilogram every three days in GPX4-knockdown xenografts and GPX4-low patient-derived xenografts, with sulfasalazine combination in GPX4-high HT1080 and liproxstatin-1 reversal.
figureAudit.readout: Tumour growth and 4-hydroxynonenal.
figureAudit.answer: Brequinar suppresses GPX4-knockdown xenografts and some GPX4-low models more strongly, the combination is required in GPX4-high tumours, and liproxstatin-1 reverses part of the effect.
figureAudit.boundary: Dosing is in immunodeficient hosts, 4-hydroxynonenal and liproxstatin-1 alone cannot exclude systemic metabolic or off-target effects, and in view of the 2023 dispute the proposed drug selectivity and GPX4 stratification are not established as translatable.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Main figure, Extended Data 1 to 9 and the 2023 Matters Arising and Reply as recorded in the project audit archive.

title: Identification of essential sites of lipid peroxidation in ferroptosis
theme: endoplasmic reticulum as an early site of lethal lipid peroxidation
conditionVector: HT-1080 and additional cancer cell lines in culture, driven by four mechanistically distinct FIN inducer classes and by FINO2-type endoperoxides, with bis-allylic dideuterated PUFAs, monounsaturated fatty acids and ACSL4 overexpression as perturbations; readouts are stimulated Raman scattering imaging of the carbon–deuterium vibration, organelle-masked C11-BODIPY oxidation, molecular-species LC-MS and subcellular fractionation over acute inducer time courses.

sixtySecond.story: Ferroptosis kills by peroxidizing membrane phospholipids, but which membrane the lethal oxidation starts in was not resolved.
sixtySecond.advance: Using deuterated fatty acids as traceable protectors and organelle-targeted endoperoxides as inducers, the work places the endoplasmic reticulum as an early and functionally important site of the peroxidation, with oxidation later extending to the plasma membrane.
sixtySecond.evidenceAnchor: Stimulated Raman imaging localizes protective D-PUFAs to the ER over lipid droplets, LC-MS and fractionation agree, organelle-redirected FINO2 analogues induce rescuable death from several compartments, and masked C11-BODIPY shows an ER-first oxidation time course under four inducer classes.
sixtySecond.scope: All work is in cultured cancer cells with chemical inducers and mobile proxy probes; a direct manipulation of ER lipid composition was attempted and did not succeed, so the ER-first ordering rests on localization rather than on causal control.
sixtySecond.openQuestion: How can ER oxidized phospholipids be manipulated and measured directly, without mobile probes or forced artificial endoperoxides, and does the compartment order shift with the inducer and cell state?

figureAudit.question: Where inside the cell do the ferroptosis-protective bis-allylic dideuterated PUFAs (D-PUFAs) mainly distribute?
figureAudit.intervention: Supplementation with several bis-allylic dideuterated PUFAs, challenged with four mechanistically distinct FIN inducer classes across multiple cell lines.
figureAudit.readout: Cell survival, and stimulated Raman scattering imaging of the carbon–deuterium vibration to localize where the labelled fatty acids accumulate.
figureAudit.answer: Several D-PUFAs suppress all four classes of ferroptosis inducer and, by stimulated Raman scattering imaging, distribute in a perinuclear and punctate pattern.
figureAudit.boundary: Where a protective agent accumulates only nominates a candidate site; the kinetic isotope effect and metabolic fate of a D-PUFA do not by themselves show that lethal peroxidation is initiated there.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive; not re-opened in this pass.

figureAudit.question: Does accumulation of D-PUFAs in lipid droplets account for their anti-ferroptotic activity?
figureAudit.intervention: Co-imaging of stimulated Raman scattering with Nile Red, lipidomic tracing of D-PUFA into triacylglycerol, and clearance of droplets with two DGAT inhibitors.
figureAudit.readout: Lipid-droplet co-localization, tracer incorporation into triacylglycerol, and cell survival.
figureAudit.answer: D-PUFAs do enter triacylglycerol and lipid droplets, but protection persists after DGAT inhibition clears the droplets, so droplets are not a required storage site in this model.
figureAudit.boundary: The conclusion is confined to acute DGAT pharmacology in HT-1080 cells; it does not extend to a claim that lipid droplets are irrelevant to ferroptosis in every cell state.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive; not re-opened in this pass.

figureAudit.question: With droplets excluded, which membranes and phospholipids do the D-PUFAs mainly enter?
figureAudit.intervention: ER and Golgi co-imaging, molecular-species LC-MS of phospholipids, and ER versus mitochondria fractionation with resident-protein contamination controls.
figureAudit.readout: Marker overlap, phospholipid species distribution, and fraction-level label enrichment.
figureAudit.answer: SRS overlaps strongly with ER markers and less with Golgi; LC-MS shows D-PUFAs entering phosphatidylethanolamine and ether phospholipids, and fractionation places more label in ER than in mitochondria.
figureAudit.boundary: The fractionation carries ER–mitochondria cross-contamination and the authors state they cannot exclude a minor mitochondrial pool; localization is not a substitute for a direct causal manipulation of ER lipid composition.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive; not re-opened in this pass.

figureAudit.question: Is ER enrichment specific to the protective D-PUFAs, or do pro- and anti-ferroptotic fatty acids use the same compartment?
figureAudit.intervention: Stimulated Raman imaging of several deuterated fatty acids, ACSL4 overexpression, and ER versus plasma-membrane masking.
figureAudit.readout: Cell survival and relative incorporation quantified per compartment.
figureAudit.answer: A pro-ferroptotic non-bis-allylic deuterated PUFA and an anti-ferroptotic MUFA both enter the ER and droplets yet move sensitivity in opposite directions, and ACSL4 overexpression strengthens the PUFA effect.
figureAudit.boundary: Shared localization with opposite effects shows that being in the ER does not by itself set direction; the specific fatty-acid chemistry, acyl activation and which phospholipid is entered matter as much.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive; not re-opened in this pass.

figureAudit.question: Can targeting endoperoxides to different organelles induce ferroptosis directly from more than one compartment?
figureAudit.intervention: Structure-directed chemistry to relocate a FINO2-type endoperoxide to the ER/Golgi, mitochondria or lysosome, with organelle-marker co-localization and dose–response under ferroptosis-inhibitor rescue.
figureAudit.readout: Fluorescence and SRS localization, cell-death dose–response, and rescue by ferrostatin-1 or D-PUFAs.
figureAudit.answer: FINO2 analogues can be redirected to the ER/Golgi, mitochondria or lysosome and, in each case, trigger death that ferrostatin-1 or D-PUFAs rescue.
figureAudit.boundary: Chemical derivatization and forced targeting may change reactivity, potency and cellular uptake at once; this shows several compartments are sufficient to carry an artificial peroxidation stress, not the natural order of events under an endogenous inducer.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive; not re-opened in this pass.

figureAudit.question: Under common ferroptosis inducers, does lipid peroxidation follow an ER-first, plasma-membrane-later time course?
figureAudit.intervention: Four FIN inducer classes, several timepoints, and ER/plasma-membrane and mitochondria/lysosome masks applied over the oxidation time course.
figureAudit.readout: C11-BODIPY oxidation ratio per compartment and the timing of morphological collapse.
figureAudit.answer: Organelle-masked C11-BODIPY shows an ER oxidation signal appearing first under all four inducer classes, with the plasma-membrane signal and morphological collapse rising close to death.
figureAudit.boundary: C11-BODIPY is a mobile proxy probe, and co-localization at a few timepoints cannot establish the ER as a universal endogenous origin; the authors' attempt to directly alter ER area and composition did not succeed, and that causal gap remains open.
figureAudit.sourceScope: Figure and legend as recorded in the project audit archive; not re-opened in this pass.
